title: Caring for Yourself While Caring for a Dying Loved One
summary: Self-care during this period often feels impossible or even inappropriate to prioritize — but it matters more than ever right now.
subcategory: Anticipatory Grief and Approaching End of Life
journeyStage: Anticipatory Grief and Approaching End of Life
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Caring for Yourself While Caring for a Dying Loved One | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Self-care during this period often feels impossible or even inappropriate to prioritize — but it matters more than ever right now.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Exhaustion during this period is real and expected, not a sign of weakness.
Accepting help from others isn't a failure to be present enough yourself.
Basic needs — sleep, food, brief breaks — genuinely affect your capacity to show up for your loved one.

paragraphs: It's common to feel guilty about stepping away, even briefly, but exhaustion during this period is entirely expected and doesn't reflect a lack of love or commitment — you cannot pour from an empty cup, however true that feels to hear right now.

Accepting help — from family, friends, or hospice volunteers — isn't a sign that you're failing to be present enough; it's often what allows you to remain present in the moments that matter most, rather than running on empty.

nextSteps: Accept offers of help, even small ones, rather than declining out of guilt.
Prioritize basic needs — sleep, food, brief breaks — as seriously as any other care task.
Reach out to the hospice social worker for caregiver-specific support resources.
disclaimer: This is genuinely hard, ongoing work, and it's alright to need support yourself right now — the hospice team's social worker can connect you with resources, including grief and caregiver support groups.

sources.name: Providing Care and Comfort at the End of Life
